attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of an intensely itchy rash. She commenced a new job as a berry picker four-months prior. She reports multiple blisters predominantly on her elbows, knees, and buttocks. The itching is severe, often causing her to scratch until the blisters break and form crusts, despite taking oral antihistamines daily for her hayfever. She has also experienced intermittent abdominal discomfort and occasional diarrhoea over the past year. She denies haematochezia or melena.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Skin examination reveals symmetrically distributed erythematous papules and small vesicles on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and knees, as well as on her buttocks. Some areas show excoriations and crusting. Her abdomen is mildly bloated and soft. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis for her presentation?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation in this case is most consistent with dermatitis herpetiformis. This condition typically presents with intensely pruritic papulovesicular lesions distributed symmetrically on extensor surfaces, particularly the elbows, knees, buttocks, and upper back. The severe itching often leads to excoriation and crusting, as seen in this patient. The associated gastrointestinal symptoms (abdominal discomfort and diarrhoea) further support this diagnosis, as dermatitis herpetiformis is strongly associated with coeliac disease. The mean onset is 50 years of age, with more men affected than women. Skin biopsy would demonstrate granular IgA deposits. Treatment for DH is a life-long gluten free diet.

Bullous pemphigoid typically affects older adults and presents with large, tense bullae on the trunk and flexor surfaces of the extremities. It is not typically associated with gastrointestinal symptoms, and the distribution of lesions differs from what is described in this case.

Scabies can cause intense itching and small papules or vesicles, but the distribution is typically different, often affecting the interdigital spaces, wrists, axillae, and genitals. The symmetrical distribution on extensor surfaces and the associated gastrointestinal symptoms make scabies less likely in this case.

Atopic dermatitis (eczema) can cause pruritic lesions, but it typically affects flexor surfaces rather than extensor surfaces. It is also more common in childhood, although it can persist into adulthood. The vesicular nature of the lesions and the distribution described are not typical for atopic dermatitis.

Contact dermatitis results from exposure to an irritant or allergen and typically occurs at the site of contact. Whilst this may occur with a new work environment in an atopic individual, the symmetrical distribution on extensor surfaces and the associated gastrointestinal symptoms make this diagnosis less likely.
learningPoints: Dermatitis herpetiformis typically presents with intensely pruritic papulovesicular lesions on extensor surfaces.
Dermatitis herpetiformis is strongly associated with celiac disease.
references: Al-Tameemi, A, Elyoussifi S, Coulson I. Dermatitis herpetiformis. Dermnet NZ. 2023. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/dermatitis-herpetiformis

Reunala T, Hervonen K, Salmi T. Dermatitis Herpetiformis: An Update on Diagnosis and Management. Am J Clin Dermatol. 2021 May;22(3):329-338. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8068693/

attributes.name: Graduating
